stories.headline: Waymo moves to employee-only driverless rides in Denver, public launch expected later in 2026
stories.summary:
Waymo announced Wednesday that its self-driving vehicles in Denver will begin operating without human safety attendants, with rides initially available only to company employees in neighborhoods including Baker, Cherry Creek and the River North Art District. The shift marks a significant milestone in Waymo's Denver rollout, which began in September when white Jaguar and blue Zeekr robotaxis started collecting data on city streets with human handlers aboard.

The company said a public launch for non-employees is expected sometime later in 2026, though no specific date has been given. Waymo also announced it will add the Ojai, a minivan-like vehicle built on Geely's electric Zeekr platform with Waymo's sixth-generation autonomous software, to its Denver fleet — designed specifically to handle snowy conditions.

Waymo cited an internal analysis of more than 220 million fully autonomous miles driven through March, claiming its vehicles were involved in 94% fewer crashes causing serious or fatal injuries compared with human drivers in the same cities. Three automated driving system crashes have been reported in Colorado, according to National Highway Traffic Safety Administration data.

Once Waymo opens its Denver service to the public, it will fall under the oversight of the Colorado Public Utilities Commission, which also regulates taxi companies. Colorado has generally been regarded as having a more permissive regulatory environment for autonomous vehicles than states such as California.

stories.headline: 18-year-old rescued from abandoned Cold War missile silo in Arapahoe County after being trapped for four hours
stories.summary:
Metro Denver search and rescue crews pulled an 18-year-old woman from an abandoned nuclear missile silo near the Arapahoe County Fairgrounds early Wednesday morning after she was trapped roughly four hours underground. Crews from Sable Altura Fire Rescue, South Metro Fire Rescue and the Arapahoe County Sheriff's Office responded around 6 a.m. after two other teenagers called 911.

Paramedics transported the woman to a hospital; her condition was described as non-emergent. The Arapahoe County Sheriff's Office is investigating the incident as a trespassing case and had not determined as of Wednesday whether the three teenagers would face citations or court summonses.

Sable Altura Fire Chief Rich Solomon warned that the decommissioned Cold War-era silos — several of which remain in Colorado — are among the most dangerous structures in the state. The silos are hundreds of feet deep, completely dark and can contain unknown gases and other hazards. Solomon noted that another teenager who fell into a similar silo in 2024 suffered life-altering injuries.

"Death is 100% on the table for people who try to enter the silos," Solomon said, adding that the structures "need to be sealed up forever."

stories.headline: Colorado county and jail healthcare provider to pay $10.2 million after man died from untreated injuries sustained in custody
stories.summary:
Huerfano County and a Pueblo-based healthcare provider have agreed to pay $10.2 million to the estate of Michael Burch, a 69-year-old man who died in the Huerfano County Detention Center in April 2023 after officers broke his ribs and failed to provide adequate medical care over eight days. A county coroner determined Burch died from complications of blunt force trauma to his chest after a detention officer tackled him into a metal bench.

Huerfano County and its sheriff's office will pay $9.2 million, while Health Care Partners Foundation Inc. — the jail's healthcare provider at the time — will pay an additional $1 million under the settlement. Burch suffered six badly broken ribs, a collapsed lung, internal bleeding and bruised intestines in the March 28, 2023 incident.

Surveillance footage showed that immediately after Burch was tackled and handcuffed, Captain Lea Vigil made a circular motion with her finger near her head and said "Batshit," reflecting an attitude that persisted as a large black bruise spread across Burch's torso over the following days. A telehealth visit five days after his injuries failed to identify the rib damage, with the nurse practitioner focusing instead on Burch's mental health.

Burch, a former correctional officer who had moved to Colorado for retirement, was in a mental health crisis at the time of his arrest. Health Care Partners Foundation CEO Rita Torres said her organization follows national standards and placed blame on the sheriff's office for blocking medical staff access to Burch, while Burch's attorneys alleged both parties bore responsibility for his death.

stories.headline: Monsoon rains on the horizon for Colorado, but critical fire conditions expected to return this weekend
stories.summary:
National Weather Service meteorologists say moisture arriving from the southwest this week is offering a brief reprieve from the hot, dry and windy conditions fueling multiple major wildfires across Colorado, though forecasters warn that critical fire conditions will return by the weekend. Experts say a true monsoon pattern — which typically arrives in mid-July — could bring more sustained relief, with some forecasters also pointing to a potentially strong El Niño developing this fall.

Colorado fire crews are currently battling several large blazes, including the approximately 93,900-acre Aspen Acres fire that has destroyed at least 263 homes in Custer and Pueblo counties, the 51,600-acre Ferris fire in Montezuma and Dolores counties, the 29,300-acre Gold Mountain fire near Ouray, and the 4,100-acre Willow fire near Leadville. Smoke from these fires, combined with smoke from blazes in other states and Canada, has been spreading across Colorado and the broader West.

State climatologist Russ Schumacher said there is now near-certainty that El Niño conditions will arrive by fall, with better-than-even odds that it will be a strong event. A "super El Niño" pattern — which climate experts gave a 70% probability of occurring as of mid-June — would impact weather worldwide and could bring additional moisture to Colorado in the fall and winter.

In the short term, National Weather Service meteorologist Cameron Simcoe urged Coloradans to take precautions to prevent human-caused ignitions, including carefully extinguishing cigarettes, avoiding dragging chains from vehicles and closely monitoring campfires where allowed.

stories.headline: Nuggets waive center Jonas Valanciunas before guarantee deadline, saving at least $8 million
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets made the expected move Wednesday of waiving veteran center Jonas Valanciunas before his contract guarantee deadline, freeing the team of at least $8 million in salary obligations. The decision closes the door on a brief stint with the organization for the 14-year NBA veteran.

The Nuggets had been reported to be in trade discussions with the Los Angeles Lakers involving Valanciunas ahead of the July 8 guarantee deadline, but ultimately chose to waive him outright. The move gives Denver additional financial flexibility as the franchise manages a luxury tax bill that had been projected at $177 million.

The Nuggets also have forward DaRon Holmes II attending NBA Summer League in Las Vegas, though he is not on the team's Summer League roster. Holmes said he is using the time to continue his rehabilitation and development rather than treating the trip as a vacation.

stories.headline: USWNT captain and Golden native Lindsey Heaps joins Denver Summit FC, set to debut July 18
stories.summary:
U.S. Women's National Team captain Lindsey Heaps officially joined Denver Summit FC this week, with the Golden native making an immediate impression on her new teammates and coaching staff after just three days of practice. Head coach Nick Cushing said her presence has already elevated the team's standards as the expansion club recovers from a 3-0 loss to Kansas City.

Heaps, 32, signed a four-year deal with the club through 2029 after four seasons with French powerhouse OL Lyonnes. She will not play in Sunday's match against the Houston Dash and is scheduled to make her Denver debut July 18 against the Portland Thorns at the opening of Centennial Stadium. Her introduction to Denver fans comes with added personal significance, as she expects her parents will be in the stands for the home opener.

General manager Curt Johnson said acquiring Heaps — who earned NWSL MVP honors in 2018 and was part of Portland's 2017 championship squad — was a yearlong process driven by her willingness to embrace the challenges of building an expansion franchise. Heaps said the ownership group's investment in facilities, including the CommonSpirit Performance Center in Centennial, convinced her the club was the right fit.

"It's such a grateful feeling," Heaps said. "I'm so happy to be here." Defender Janine Sonis, who previously wore the captain's armband, will serve as vice captain alongside Heaps.

stories.headline: Rockies Triple-A pitcher Domingo Acevedo suspended 80 games for positive PED test
stories.summary:
Right-handed pitcher Domingo Acevedo, a three-year major league veteran currently pitching for the Colorado Rockies' Triple-A affiliate, the Albuquerque Isotopes, was suspended 80 games Wednesday after testing positive for stanozolol, an anabolic steroid, under Minor League Baseball's drug policy. The suspension is effective immediately.

Acevedo, 30, has appeared at the major league level during his career but was working to earn a roster spot with the Rockies organization at the time of the positive test. The 80-game suspension is the standard penalty for a first positive test for a performance-enhancing substance under the minor league program.

The suspension is a further setback for the Rockies, who are already navigating uncertainty heading into the MLB trade deadline with a roster that has struggled throughout the 2026 season.

stories.headline: Westminster's Trimble Inc. retains Goldman Sachs to sell transportation and logistics unit
stories.summary:
Westminster-based Trimble Inc. has retained Goldman Sachs to find a buyer for its transportation and logistics business division, which generated $549.2 million in revenue in 2025, according to reports. The move signals a significant strategic shift for the positioning technology company, which has been restructuring its portfolio in recent years.

Trimble, headquartered in Westminster, develops technology and software solutions used across industries including agriculture, construction, geospatial and transportation. The transportation and logistics unit provides fleet management, routing and compliance solutions widely used by trucking and logistics companies.

A sale of the division, if completed, would represent one of the larger corporate divestitures tied to the Denver metro area's technology sector in recent memory. No sale price or timeline has been reported, and Goldman Sachs had not publicly commented on the engagement as of Wednesday.

stories.headline: Former Channel 7 lot near downtown Denver listed as 'available' as developer seeks new partners for stalled apartment project
stories.summary:
The 2.3-acre lot at Speer Boulevard and Lincoln Street — where the former KMGH Channel 7 building stood until its recent demolition — has been placed on the market, with a sign advertising the property as "available" now visible on fencing surrounding the cleared site. The property was formally listed in February, according to an online listing.

New York-based developer Property Markets Group (PMG) and Toronto's Greybrook Realty Partners, who purchased the property in 2021 for $35 million, said they are primarily seeking new investment partners to help finance the next phase of development rather than an outright sale. "We are exploring options for new partners to benefit from the project's next phase of development," PMG said in a statement, while declining to rule out selling its full stake.

The project, called Society Denver, was originally planned as two towers totaling nearly 600 apartment units before developers scaled back plans in 2024 to an 11-story, 481-unit complex to comply with height limits protecting views of the Rocky Mountains. The site sits in the Golden Triangle, where more than 8,000 residential units have been built or are in the planning stages within a half-mile radius.

The listing comes as Denver's apartment development market has slowed significantly following an oversupply period that pushed vacancies higher and reduced rents across the metro area.

stories.headline: Skeletal remains found by gold panner along Wheat Ridge creek in 2024 identified as Denver-area man missing for a decade
stories.summary:
Human skeletal remains discovered by a gold panner at the Arapahoe Bar Gold Panning Park in Wheat Ridge in December 2024 have been identified as Robbie Swor, a man born in 1968 whose family had not heard from him in roughly 10 years, the Jefferson County Coroner's Office confirmed. The park sits along Clear Creek about four miles east of Golden.

Identification proved difficult because the remains were fully skeletal. A breakthrough came when a bone fragment was sent to Othram Inc., a Texas-based genetic genealogy firm, which built a DNA profile and identified relatives of the deceased. Coroner's investigator Chandler Ellis then traced the family tree from the late 1800s in Syria through descendants living in Colorado, eventually connecting the remains to Swor, who was last known to be in the Denver area.

A close relative provided a DNA sample that confirmed the identification. Swor will be cremated and his ashes returned to his family. The case required more than a year of work by the Jefferson County Coroner's Office, the Wheat Ridge Police Department and the Lakewood Police Department.

The case highlights the growing role of forensic genetic genealogy in identifying unidentified remains across Colorado and the country.

stories.headline: Santa Fe Drive in Denver to close this weekend for new bridge installation between Evans and Florida avenues
stories.summary:
Santa Fe Drive in Denver will be closed this weekend to accommodate the installation of a new bridge, affecting travel between Evans and Florida avenues. The closure is expected to cause significant traffic disruptions in the area, which is a major north-south corridor connecting the city's southern neighborhoods to downtown.

Drivers are advised to plan alternate routes during the closure period. The project is part of ongoing infrastructure work in the corridor. Specific detour routes and the full duration of the closure were not immediately detailed in available reports.

stories.headline: Wildlife study finds billionaire's 8-foot fence at San Luis Valley ranch harms 30 species; owner rejects negotiations
stories.summary:
A wildlife study examining an 8-foot fence erected around a billionaire's ranch in Colorado's San Luis Valley has found that the barrier negatively impacts at least 30 species by restricting movement across the landscape. The findings add scientific weight to growing concerns among wildlife managers and conservation advocates about the ecological effects of the fence.

Despite the study's conclusions, the ranch owner has rejected overtures to negotiate modifications to the fence that might allow wildlife passage. The San Luis Valley, a high-altitude basin in south-central Colorado, supports diverse wildlife including pronghorn, mule deer and elk that depend on seasonal migration corridors.

The dispute reflects a broader tension in rural Colorado between private property rights and the public interest in wildlife connectivity across large landscapes. State wildlife managers and neighboring landowners have been watching the situation closely, given the fence's potential to disrupt migration patterns that cross property boundaries.

joke.joke: There is one thing I would break up over, and that is if she caught me with another woman. I wouldn't stand for that.

topStory.headline: U.S. and Iran Exchange Strikes for Second Consecutive Day as Fighting Spreads Across Gulf Region
topStory.summary:
The United States and Iran traded military strikes for a second straight day Thursday as fighting intensified over control of the Strait of Hormuz, the critical waterway through which a significant share of the world's oil supply passes. U.S. Central Command stated the latest American strikes were aimed at degrading Iran's ability to threaten freedom of navigation in the strait, citing recent Iranian attacks on commercial shipping. Iranian state media reported the strikes killed three people and wounded several others.

Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps responded swiftly, announcing retaliatory strikes against U.S. military installations at four bases across Kuwait and Bahrain. The Iranian Army subsequently said it had also targeted sites in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Qatar using one-way attack drones, according to Iranian state television. Missile alert sirens sounded in Jordan's capital, Amman, and in other areas of the kingdom as the conflict spread beyond the immediate theater of operations.

The exchanges came on the same day Iran buried its slain Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was killed on February 28 in joint Israeli and American airstrikes. Khamenei was interred at the shrine city of Mashhad in northeastern Iran, following a week of mass funeral processions and mourning ceremonies across the country. His son and designated successor, Mojtaba Khamenei, remained hidden from public view, reportedly having been disfigured in the strike that killed his father.

Adding further tension to the day's events, an Iranian official accused U.S. forces of conducting a strike near the Bushehr nuclear power plant, Iran's only operating nuclear facility, hours after U.S. Central Command declared it had concluded its strikes for the day. American officials did not immediately acknowledge the allegation. A separate diplomatic track appeared to remain open in Lebanon, where a U.S. delegation was expected to arrive to oversee the implementation of an Israeli military withdrawal from designated pilot zones in southern Lebanon, according to the Lebanese presidency.
